This listing is for 1 plant that is 6"- 8" tall. This grafted variety has a lot to offer! It stands out within the "Ghost series" style of maples for its highly vibrant, almost glowing spring coloration and intense structural definition. To maintain the brilliant "frosted" look without getting leaf scorch, give 'Frosted Purple' morning sun and afternoon shade. Variegated and Ghost-type maples perform best when protected from the harsh, direct afternoon heat.

Foliage & Reticulation: In the spring, the serrated, jagged leaves emerge a very bright, light pink-purple or deep pink with stark, dark purple veins. This prominent patterning—where the veins contrast sharply against the rest of the leaf blade—is known as reticulation.  

Summer & Fall Shifts: As summer progresses, the color mellows slightly, showing cream-white or frosted pink variegation over the purple-green base, though its reticulated veins hold strong. In autumn, it transitions to a rich, fiery red or red-purple.  

Size & Growth Habit: It is an upright, sculptural, and somewhat columnar grower. It reaches about 6 to 8 feet tall and 4 to 6 feet wide in its first 10 years, eventually maxing out around 10 to 12 feet, making it excellent for smaller gardens or container planting.  

Soil: Requires well-draining, slightly acidic soil. Like most Japanese maples, it is highly sensitive to "wet feet" (saturated, soggy soil), which can quickly lead to root rot.

Common Name: 'Frosted Purple' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Morning sun/Afternoon shade
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 6'-8' tall and 4'-6' wide
Bloom Color: N/A
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous

This listing is for 1 live high grafted tree that is 8"- 10" tall. Another beautiful dwarf red laceleaf weeping cultivar! This cultivar is widely considered one of the best cascading, laceleaf Japanese maples you can grow. The name translates beautifully from Japanese as "leaves like a cascading rice tassel," which perfectly describes its weeping habit. What makes this specific cultivar stand out from other red dissectums (like 'Tamukeyama' or 'Crimson Queen') is its exceptional color retention and slightly more robust, upright-then-weeping growth pattern.

Foliage: It features large, deeply divided, intricate lace-like leaves. The color emerges as a brilliant, deep purple-red in the spring.

Summer Heat Resistance: While many red Japanese maples "green out" or scorch during hot summer months, 'Inaba shidare' holds onto its dark burgundy-purple hues remarkably well, even in full sun.

Autumn Show: In the fall, the dark canopy shifts dramatically into a fiery, bright crimson-red.

Growth Habit: It forms a cascading mound that tends to grow slightly taller and faster than other weeping varieties, giving it a strong architectural presence in landscapes or large containers.

Mature Size: 6 to 10 feet tall and 8 to 12 feet wide
Sun Exposure: Full sun to partial shade (performs best with afternoon shade in hot climates)
Soil Requirements: Moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; intolerant of wet feet

Common Name: 'Inaba Shidare' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum dissectum
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Full sun/part shade
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 6' to 10' tall and 8' to 12' wide

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 6"- 8" tall. This is a gorgeous yellow bark cultivar is a spectacular, prized cultivar loved primarily for its striking, architectural winter interest. Its name fittingly translates from Japanese to English as "beautiful mountain range" or "precious jewel."
While classic coral-bark maples like 'Sango kaku' dominate gardens with bright red winter branches, 'Bihou' offers a luminous alternative with its vivid, almost glowing bark.

The Bark: During the spring and summer, the bark is a pleasant, clean green-gold. As temperatures drop in autumn and winter, the magic happens: the young branches and twigs transform into an intense, glowing apricot-yellow to golden-orange, sometimes blushing with soft pink highlights on the sides exposed to direct sunlight.

The Foliage: In spring, the chartreuse leaves emerge with subtle reddish-orange margins. They mature into a bright, clean medium green for the summer months.

Fall Color: In autumn, the foliage puts on a final show, turning brilliant shades of yellow, gold, and deep orange, perfectly complementing the changing color of the wood beneath.

This tree is a relatively fast grower when young, establishing an upright, vase-shaped structure. Over time, it softens into a beautifully rounded, dense small tree.

Height: 10 to 14 feet at maturity.

Width: 6 to 10 feet wide.

Suitability: Its compact, upright silhouette makes it an exceptional choice for smaller urban gardens, focal entryways, or as a high-contrast understory tree.

Sunlight: It performs best in dappled shade or morning sun with afternoon protection. While full morning sun intensifies the winter bark coloration, harsh, intense afternoon sun can scorch its bright leaves.

Soil: Like most Japanese maples, it demands well-draining, moist, and acidic soil rich in organic matter. It will not tolerate heavy, soggy clay ("wet feet").

What makes this tree so sought after is its dramatic, "wild" color transformation throughout the year:
Spring: The showstopper season. Leaves emerge with a bright, hot pink and magenta border surrounding a purplish-green center. The contrast is incredibly vivid.
Summer: The pink edges soften into a creamy white or pastel pink variegation, while the center of the leaf shifts to a deeper green, occasionally splashed with white.
Autumn: The tree wraps up the growing season with a brilliant display of orange-to-purple-red fire.

This is a slower-growing, compact dwarf tree with an upright, bushy habit.
Height: Expect it to reach about 6 feet tall and 4 feet wide over a 10-year period.
Containers: Because of its small stature and controlled growth, it is a perfect candidate for large patio containers or small courtyard gardens.

Light: Morning sun with afternoon shade is highly recommended. The delicate pink and white variegated edges are susceptible to leaf scorch if exposed to intense, direct afternoon sunlight. However, too much deep shade will cause the vibrant colors to fade to a dull green.
Soil: Like most Japanese maples, it thrives in moist, well-draining, slightly acidic soil. It hates having "wet feet" (waterlogged roots), so ensure proper drainage whether it is in the ground or a pot.

This listing is for 1 plant that is 6"- 8" tall. This is a rare, highly sought-after dwarf Japanese maple that is an absolute favorite for small gardens, containers, and bonsai enthusiasts. The name Goshiki translates to "five colors," hinting at the incredible changing canvas this tree provides throughout the seasons. What makes 'Goshiki kotohime' truly unique is the way its leaves grow. The tiny, ruffled, five-lobed leaves grow incredibly tight and clustered along the upright branches. This dense, congested growth gives the stems a distinct, textured look—often described as looking like pipe cleaners or a bottle brush.

Size (at 10 years): Roughly 3 to 6 feet tall and 2 to 3 feet wide. It maintains a very narrow, upright, V-shaped structure.  Growth Rate: Extremely slow, averaging only 4 to 8 inches of new growth per year.

Spring: This is the tree's peak show. New growth emerges with a glowing soft orange or reddish tint, quickly shifting into a beautiful, impressionistic mix of bright pink, cream, white, and light green variegation.  
Summer: The variegation gradually subdues as the leaves mature into a rich, handsome kelly green, though the tightly packed leaf arrangement keeps it looking highly textured. 
Fall: The foliage transitions into a striking palette of bright gold, yellow-orange, and orange-red.

Sunlight: Morning sun with afternoon shade is the absolute sweet spot. Variegated leaves are highly susceptible to leaf scorch if exposed to intense, hot afternoon sun, especially in warmer climates.  Soil: It requires rich, consistently moist, but well-drained soil. Like all Japanese maples, they absolutely despise "wet feet" (standing water), which can cause root rot.  
Watering: Water regularly to maintain even moisture. Do not let the soil dry out completely. Applying a layer of mulch around the base will help retain moisture and keep the root system cool.  Pruning: Minimal pruning is required due to its dwarf nature. If you do need to shape it or remove dead wood, only prune in the summer after the leaves have completely unfurled. Pruning in late winter or spring will cause the tree to "bleed" sap heavily.

Common Name: 'Goshiki Kotohime' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Full sun/part sun
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 3' - 6' tall and 2' - 3' wide
Bloom Color: N/A
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous

This listing is for 1 live plant that is 6"-8" tall and multi-branched. This beautiful variety is a highly popular, resilient bigleaf mophead variety. It stands out as an exceptional choice for home landscapes due to its unique blooming habits and reliable cold hardiness.

Reblooming Power (Remontant): Unlike traditional bigleaf hydrangeas that only bloom once on "old wood" (the previous year's stems), 'Penny Mac' blooms on both old and new wood. This means even if a harsh winter kills the branches down to the ground, the new shoots that emerge in the spring will still produce beautiful flower clusters late in the summer.

Soil Type	              Soil pH	              Bloom Color	                                  How to Achieve It
Highly Acidic	Less than 5.5	Vibrant Blue / Purple	Add aluminum sulfate to the soil in spring.
Alkaline	             Greater than 6.5	Clear Pink / Rose               Add garden lime to the soil in spring

Common Name: "Penny Mac" Hydrangea
Botanical Name: Hydrangea macrophylla
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: Summer
Sunlight: Part sun
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 4' - 6' tall and wide
Bloom Color: Depends on soil PH
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 8"- 10" tall. A Ghost Series cultivar that is an incredible, dwarf addition to the famous "Ghost Series" of Japanese maples. If you love dramatic, shifting seasonal colors but have limited space, this one is an absolute standout. Like its larger relatives (such as 'Amber Ghost' or 'Sister Ghost'), 'Baby Ghost' features striking reticulated variegation. This means the leaf veins display a sharply contrasting color against the main leaf tissue.

Spring: Leaves emerge with bright tones of fiery red, orange, and pink, with darker, prominent veining showing through.

Summer: The foliage transitions to soft greens and bronze shades, though the distinct vein patterns remain visible.

Autumn: It puts on a spectacular finale with vibrant, blazing orange to deep red tones.

Size & Habit: This is a true dwarf variety. It typically reaches only about 4 to 5 feet tall and wide over 10 years, growing into a dense, beautifully rounded, bushy shrub. Its compact nature makes it absolutely perfect for container gardening, patios, or small courtyard gardens.

Light: It thrives best in partial shade. Morning sun followed by afternoon shade is ideal. Too much hot, direct afternoon sun can scorch the delicate, variegated leaves, while full shade may cause the distinct reticulated patterns to fade into a solid green.

Soil: Plant it in well-draining, slightly acidic soil. Japanese maples hate having "wet feet," so ensure excellent drainage whether it is in the ground or a pot.

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 6"- 8" tall. This is a gorgeous Red Lion dwarf cultivar and is a spectacular, highly structural dwarf cultivar. It is a witch's broom mutation found on the popular green 'Shishigashira' (Lion's Head) maple, meaning it inherits that signature tightly crinkled, bunched-up foliage but adds intense red coloration. Because of its compact, slow growth and exceptionally dense, gnarled branching pattern, 'Kurenai jishi' is also a highly prized choice for bonsai enthusiasts.

Foliage Texture: The leaves are small, deeply divided, and uniquely crinkled or curled, growing in dense, compact tufts along short, stiff branches. This gives the tree an incredibly architectural, texture-heavy look.

Summer: Matures to a deep burgundy-purple. In hot climates, it may soften to a bronzy-green or dark maroon.

Autumn: Puts on a dramatic show of brilliant orange to fiery red.

Size & Growth Habit: It is a slow-growing dwarf with a naturally upright, multi-stemmed, and bushy form. Expect it to reach only about 4 to 5 feet tall and 3 feet wide over a 10-year period, making it perfect for smaller gardens, rockeries, or large containers.

Light: Throws its best color in full sun to partial shade. In scorching hot climates, afternoon filtered shade is highly recommended to prevent the tightly packed leaf tips from burning.

This listing is for 1 plant that is 8"- 10" tall. This variegated grafted variety has a lot to offer! This is a highly sought-after, collector-grade dwarf Japanese maple. It is famous for its heavily reticulated leaves (meaning they have prominent, contrasting veins) and shifting neon colors.

This tree acts like living art, shifting dramatically depending on the month:
Spring: Leaves emerge a luminous, intense strawberry-pink to bright pinkish-red, heavily accented with deep burgundy or purple veins.
Summer: The "frosted" look takes over. The older leaves soften to a creamy white or pale mint-green background with distinct red-to-green veins. Meanwhile, hot weather triggers a second flush of new growth that pops as bright pinkish-red over the frosted older canopy.  
Fall: The foliage turns to a fiery finale of bright yellow-orange, rust, and glowing scarlet red before dropping for winter.

"Avoid heavy fertilizing. Variegated and reticulated Japanese maples like 'Olsen's Frosted Strawberry' can actually lose their beautiful variegation and revert to plain green if they are given too much nitrogen fertilizer. If planted in the ground, they rarely need any supplemental feeding at all."

Sun Exposure: Morning Sun / Afternoon Shade (Crucial). The delicate, variegated leaves need morning light to bring out the pinks, but intense afternoon sun will easily scorch and crisp the foliage.
Soil Type: Rich, organic, slightly acidic soil.
Drainage: Excellent drainage is mandatory. Like most Japanese maples, it absolutely hates "wet feet" (soggy, saturated soil), which easily leads to root rot.
Watering: Keep the soil consistently moist but not waterlogged. Container-grown trees will need much more frequent watering as pots dry out quickly.

Common Name: 'Olsen's Frosted Strawberry' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Full sun/part sun
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 5'-6' tall and 4'-6' wide
Bloom Color: N/A
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 10" - 12" tall. This is another rare fabulous grafted variegated variety with non-stop color! If you are looking for a show-stopping focal point, this tree delivers a striking display of colors across the seasons. 

The Foliage: The leaves are heavily divided, narrow, and often slightly sickle-shaped or curled.
High Stability: Many variegated red Japanese maples are notorious for "reverting" back to solid green or solid red. 'Lileeanne's Jewel' is highly celebrated because its multi-colored patterns remain exceptionally stable year after year.
Spring: Leaves emerge a vibrant burgundy-red heavily splashed and mottled with hot pink.
Summer: The base color shifts to a deeper burgundy-green, while the variegation cools into a beautiful matrix of pink, cream-white, and green.
Fall: A final explosion of bright crimson reds deeply infused with electric pink variegation.

Size & Growth Habit: It develops a small, upright, slightly bushy or mounded shape. It grows at a slow-to-moderate rate, typically reaching 8 to 10 feet tall and 5 to 6 feet wide over 10 to 15 years. This compact size makes it excellent for smaller garden beds or large patio containers.
Sun Exposure: It does best in morning sun with afternoon shade (or all-day dappled light). While it is noted for being fairly heat-tolerant with minimal leaf scorch compared to older variegated varieties, the pink and white margins can still burn if subjected to punishing afternoon sun in hotter climates.

Golden Rule for Care: Do not over-fertilize. Heavy chemical fertilizers can push out rapid growth that temporary dilutes or overrides the variegation. Stick to organic-rich, well-draining soil and a light layer of mulch to keep the roots cool. If any rare solid-red or solid-green branches appear, simply prune them out to preserve the tree's patterned look.

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 10" - 12" tall. Another beautiful cultivar in the Ghost Series! Like its siblings, 'Uncle Ghost' is celebrated for its spectacular reticulated variegation—meaning the primary veins of the leaf remain a sharp, contrasting color while the tissue between them changes dramatically throughout the seasons.
The Golden Rule for Ghost Maples: Protection from the afternoon sun is non-negotiable. Because the variegated sections have very little chlorophyll, hot, direct afternoon sunlight will quickly scorch the leaf margins.

Spring: Leaves emerge in a striking flush of bright rose-pink, peach, or soft crimson. The inner leaf tissue quickly transitions into a cream or amber wash, while the prominent emerald or lime-green veins remain deeply etched.

Summer: The bright pinks mature into olive-green and soft cream tones with red-purple highlights on the tips, giving the entire canopy an ethereal, hazy appearance.

Fall: The tree puts on a classic, fiery finale, shifting to bright golden-yellows, oranges, and deep crimsons.

Sunlight: Give it morning sun and afternoon shade (or bright, dappled woodland light). This keeps the delicate cream and pink tones from burning while ensuring the colors stay vibrant.
Soil: Plant in rich, organic, well-draining soil that is slightly acidic (pH\ 5.5 - 6.5). Like all Japanese maples, they absolutely hate "wet feet" and will quickly decline in saturated or heavy clay soils.
Watering: Maintain consistent, even moisture. Deeply water once or twice a week, increasing the frequency during dry summer spells. Applying a thick layer of organic mulch around the root zone helps regulate temperature and conserve moisture (just keep it a couple of inches away from the trunk).

Common Name: 'Uncle Ghost' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum 
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Full sun/part sun
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 6' to 10' tall and wide
Bloom Color: N/A
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 8" - 10" tall. Another beautiful variety with variegated leaves, plus it's cold tolerant! It is a highly sought-after, collectable Japanese maple (Acer palmatum) famous for its striking, multi-colored variegation. The name translates beautifully from Japanese as "brocade clouds," which perfectly captures the mottled, cloud-like patterns on its leaves. Unlike some variegated maples that feature distinct borders or blocks of color, 'Kumoi nishiki' is known for its freckled, speckled, and mottled variegation. No two leaves are exactly alike.

Size: It is a moderate, upright grower, typically reaching 8 to 12 feet tall and about 6 to 8 feet wide over 10 to 15 years.

Shape: It forms a dense, well-branched, small tree or large shrub with a rounded canopy.

Containers & Small Gardens: Because of its compact, slow-to-moderate growth rate, it makes a phenomenal focal point for small courtyards, Japanese-style gardens, or as a long-term container specimen.

Spring: Leaves emerge with a vibrant blend of soft green, creamy white, and pale yellow, often heavily flushed with bright pink or soft orange highlights on the tips.

Summer: The variegation matures into a crisp green-and-white flecked pattern. The variegation holds up remarkably well in summer compared to other cultivars, provided it doesn't get scorched.

Autumn: The tree puts on a dramatic finale, transforming into rich shades of orange, yellow, and deep crimson. The white variegated spots often turn a brilliant orange-pink color.

Light: Give it dappled shade or morning sun with afternoon protection. Too much direct, harsh afternoon sun will scorch the delicate white parts of the leaves. Too much deep shade, and you will lose that brilliant mottled variegation, causing the leaves to revert to a solid, dull green.

Soil: Like most Japanese maples, it thrives in moist, rich, slightly acidic, and exceptionally well-drained soil. They hate having "wet feet" (standing water).

Mulching: Apply a shallow layer of mulch around the base to keep the shallow root system cool and retain moisture during hot summer spells.

Spring: Leaves emerge a bright, yellowish-green, often with delicate, faint red margins along the edges.

Texture: Because the nodes (the spaces between leaves) are incredibly short, the tree has a highly compact, tufted texture. The older bark develops a rough, aged look relatively early in its life.

Sunlight: Thrives in partial shade. It loves morning sun but needs protection from harsh, direct afternoon heat to keep its delicate leaf tips from scorching.

Soil: Requires moist, organically rich, and sharply well-draining soil. Like most Japanese maples, it hates sitting in soggy, compacted dirt.

Sun Exposure: It does best in morning sun with afternoon shade, or all-day dappled/filtered sunlight. Protecting it from harsh, intense late-afternoon summer sun prevents the delicate leaf tips from scorching.

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 8"- 12" tall. A rare and hard to find green bark dwarf cultivar! This cultivar is a highly sought-after, semi-dwarf cultivar loved for its striking color transformations and year-round visual appeal. Originating as a "witches' broom" mutation on the larger 'Aoyagi' cultivar in Australia, it offers all the best features of its parent tree but in a much tighter, more compact form.  Because of its smaller stature and slow-to-moderate growth rate, it is also a popular candidate for bonsai enthusiasts who want to showcase the brilliant green winter bark.

Four-Season Color Shifts:
Spring: Leaves emerge as a vibrant, glowing yellow-green or chartreuse.
Summer: The foliage matures into a crisp, bright lime-green.
Fall: The tree puts on a spectacular final show, turning a deep, rich golden-yellow to soft orange.
Winter: Once the leaves drop, the tree remains a focal point due to its intense, bright pea-green bark and stems.

Size & Habit: It features a dense, upright, vase-shaped growth habit. It typically matures to around 6 to 10 feet tall and 5 to 6 feet wide over 10 to 15 years, making it an excellent choice for smaller yards, rock gardens, or large patio containers.

Sun Exposure: Morning sun with afternoon shade is ideal. Full afternoon sun can cause the leaf tips to scorch or "crinkle."
Watering: Regular, consistent watering. It needs extra moisture during hot summer spells, especially if grown in a pot.
Soil Preference: Rich, moist, well-draining, and slightly acidic soil. Avoid heavy, waterlogged clay.
Maintenance: Very low. Pruning is rarely needed except to remove dead wood, which should only be done in late winter while the tree is dormant.

Common Name: 'Shidava Gold' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum 
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Part sun/part sun
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 6' to 10' tall and 5' to 6' wide
Bloom Color: N/A
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous

This listing is for 1 live grafted tree that is 10" - 12" tall. This is a thread leaf variety that packs a visual punch! This is a stunning, relatively rare dwarf cultivar of the Japanese Maple family (Acer palmatum). It belongs to the linearilobum group, often referred to as "strap-leaf" or "bamboo-leaf" maples because their leaf lobes are incredibly narrow and delicate.  If you are a collector or looking for a distinct visual anchor for a small garden space, this tree is a showstopper.

The Foliage: The strap-like leaves are highly irregular. Some emerge incredibly thin, almost like threads, while others are slightly wider. They tend to curl subtly, giving the tree a slightly wild, texturized look that lives up to its "wind-swept" name.
Color Transformation: 
Spring/Summer: Depending on the exact lineage and sun exposure, they emerge with deep burgundy tones or soft greens. By mid-summer, they mature into a handsome grayish-green or bright lime green. A second flush of growth later in the season often brings striking peach, copper, or apricot-colored leaves at the tips. 
Fall: The foliage transitions into a brilliant, fiery palette ranging from bright yellows to deep crimson reds. 
The Bark: Fukinagashi features distinct dark purple to almost-black twigs and bark. This dark skeleton creates a gorgeous, high-contrast backdrop that makes the lighter summer foliage visually pop. 
Size & Shape: This is a true dwarf variety. It has a compact, upright growth habit, typically reaching only 6 to 7 feet tall and 4 to 5 feet wide after 10 years. Because of its small footprint and slower growth rate, it is an absolute dream for container gardening or tight patio spaces.

Sun Exposure: Dappled sun to partial shade (Protect from harsh afternoon sun)
Watering: Even moisture; regular watering without waterlogging

Common Name: 'Fukinagashi' Japanese Maple
Botanical Name: Acer palmatum
Hardiness Zones: 5 - 9
Bloom Time: N/A
Sunlight: Full sun/part sun
Soil Requirements: Moist, well drained soil
Mature Size: 6' to 7' tall and 4' to 5' wide
Bloom Color: N/A
Deciduous or Evergreen: Deciduous
